agent_output: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier company (3 engineers) needing reliable, multi-channel delivery notifications with 5-minute latency tolerance, Option B (event-driven with a queue) is the clear winner. The latency budget removes the only advantage of synchronous calls, while the queue provides natural retry semantics, channel extensibility, and failure isolation — all critical requirements. However, the team size constraint means we must choose a managed queue (SQS, Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on Redis) rather than self-hosted Kafka or RabbitMQ. The architecture should be a simple delivery service publishing events to a managed queue, with per-channel notification workers consuming from it.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Owns delivery lifecycle. Publishes domain events (DeliveryStatusChanged) when a delivery transitions state. Knows nothing about notification channels.","boundaries":"Bounded by delivery domain only. Does not import or reference any notification logic. Publishes events through a thin EventPublisher interface."},{"name":"Event Queue (Managed)","responsibility":"Durable message broker that guarantees at-least-once delivery of domain events to notification workers. Handles retry with backoff, dead-letter routing, and visibility timeout.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. Accessed only through a thin adapter. AWS SQS, Google Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on managed Redis are appropriate choices for a 3-person team."},{"name":"Notification Router","responsibility":"Consumes domain events from the queue, resolves customer notification preferences (which channels), and fans out to per-channel dispatchers. This is a single lightweight worker process, not a separate service.","boundaries":"Owns the mapping from event type + customer preferences to channel dispatch. Does NOT own channel-specific formatting or API integration."},{"name":"Channel Dispatchers (Email, SMS, future: Push, WhatsApp)","responsibility":"Each dispatcher owns the integration with one external provider (SendGrid, Twilio, etc.), including message formatting, API calls, auth, and provider-specific error handling.","boundaries":"One module per channel. Stateless. Receives a normalized NotificationPayload and returns success/failure. Adding a new channel means adding one new dispatcher module — no changes to router or delivery service."},{"name":"Dead Letter Queue + Alert","responsibility":"Captures notifications that exhaust all retry attempts. Triggers an alert (PagerDuty, Slack webhook) so the team knows a customer was not notified.","boundaries":"Operational boundary. Messages here require manual investigation or automated re-drive."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Event Queue","contract":"Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged events: {deliveryId, customerId, status, timestamp, metadata}. Fire-and-forget from the service perspective.","coupling_type":"loose — delivery service depends only on an EventPublisher interface, not on queue implementation"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Notification Router","contract":"At-least-once delivery with visibility timeout. Router must be idempotent (use deliveryId + status as deduplication key).","coupling_type":"loose — queue is infrastructure, router polls or subscribes"},{"from":"Notification Router","to":"Channel Dispatchers","contract":"Calls dispatcher.send(NotificationPayload): {recipientId, channel, templateKey, templateData}. Returns {success: boolean, retryable: boolean, errorCode?}.","coupling_type":"loose — router depends on a Dispatcher interface; dispatchers are registered via a simple channel registry (map of channelType → dispatcher)"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Dead Letter Queue","contract":"Messages exceeding maxRetries are automatically routed to DLQ by the queue infrastructure.","coupling_type":"infrastructure-level — no application code needed"}],"data_flow":"1. Delivery service transitions a delivery to a new status → 2. Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged event to queue → 3. Notification Router consumes the event → 4. Router looks up customer notification preferences → 5. Router calls appropriate Channel Dispatcher(s) → 6. Dispatcher calls external provider API → 7. On failure: message returns to queue with backoff → 8. After max retries: message goes to DLQ and team is alerted","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Option B)","rationale":"The 5-minute latency tolerance eliminates synchronous advantages. A queue gives us: (1) natural retry with backoff — critical for the 'never silently drop' requirement, (2) channel decoupling — adding WhatsApp means adding a dispatcher, not modifying the delivery service, (3) failure isolation — if Twilio is down, email still works, (4) temporal decoupling — delivery service never blocks on slow provider APIs."},{"pattern":"Strategy Pattern for Channel Dispatchers","rationale":"Each channel is a strategy implementing a common Dispatcher interface. The Notification Router selects strategies based on customer preferences. Adding a channel is adding a class and registering it — Open/Closed Principle."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern (recommended addition)","rationale":"To prevent the dual-write problem (delivery status saved to DB but event lost, or vice versa), write the event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the delivery status change. A lightweight poller publishes outbox rows to the queue. This guarantees no events are silently lost — critical for the reliability requirement. For a 3-person team, a simple polling outbox (every 5-10 seconds) is sufficient; no need for CDC."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"After N retries, undeliverable messages go to a DLQ rather than being discarded. This is the safety net that guarantees the 'never silently dropped' requirement. Combined with a simple alert, this is operationally cheap."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Consumer","rationale":"At-least-once delivery means duplicates are possible. Using (deliveryId, status, channel) as an idempotency key prevents customers from receiving duplicate notifications. Simple DB check or in-memory cache with TTL."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, states: picked_up, in_transit, out_for_delivery, delivered, failed_attempt)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, tracks: channel, status, attempts, lastError)","CustomerPreferences (root: customerId, tracks: preferred channels, contact info per channel)"]},{"name":"Channel Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Stateless — no aggregates. Pure adapter layer wrapping external provider APIs."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Delivery","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Delivery publishes domain events; Notification consumes them. Delivery team defines the event schema. Notification team conforms to it but owns all notification logic."},{"upstream":"Notification","downstream":"Channel Integration","relationship":"Open Host Service. Notification defines the Dispatcher interface (the 'host'). Channel integrations conform to it. Each provider adapter is replaceable without affecting the notification context."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Option B (event-driven) over Option A (synchronous REST)","gains":"Retry is handled by queue infrastructure (not application code). Channels are decoupled — adding one is isolated. Delivery service is never blocked by slow/failing providers. Partial failures are graceful (email works even if SMS is down). Natural batching possible if volume grows.","sacrifices":"More moving parts than direct REST calls. Requires understanding message queue semantics (visibility timeout, DLQ, idempotency). Slightly harder to debug end-to-end (need correlation IDs and structured logging).","alternatives_considered":["Option A: Synchronous REST — simpler to build initially but retry logic becomes complex application code, delivery service couples to every channel, adding a channel means modifying the delivery service, provider outages block delivery processing. Rejected because the reliability requirement makes the retry problem hard to solve well in-process.","Hybrid: Synchronous with async fallback — adds complexity of both patterns without the clean benefits of either. Rejected for a 3-person team."]},{"decision":"Managed queue (SQS/BullMQ) over self-hosted broker (Kafka/RabbitMQ)","gains":"Zero operational overhead for the queue itself. No cluster management, no partition rebalancing, no disk monitoring. A 3-person team stays focused on business logic.","sacrifices":"Vendor lock-in to the chosen queue. Less control over advanced features (exactly-once, stream replay). SQS has 256KB message limit.","alternatives_considered":["Kafka — powerful but operationally heavy for 3 engineers and this volume. Overkill.","RabbitMQ — good middle ground but still requires cluster management, monitoring, upgrades. Unnecessary burden."]},{"decision":"Outbox pattern over direct publish-then-save","gains":"Guarantees event publication if the DB write succeeds. Eliminates the dual-write problem. No distributed transactions needed.","sacrifices":"Adds a polling loop and an outbox table. Slightly more code. Adds latency equal to poll interval (5-10s, well within the 5-minute budget).","alternatives_considered":["Publish-then-save — risk of DB failure after publish (customer notified about a status that wasn't saved). Unacceptable.","Save-then-publish — risk of publish failure after save (silent notification drop). Violates reliability requirement.","CDC (Debezium) — powerful but operationally complex for 3 engineers. Overkill."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Channel Dispatchers","evolution_path":"Adding push notifications or WhatsApp means: (1) implement Dispatcher interface for the new channel, (2) register it in the channel registry, (3) add it as an option in CustomerPreferences. No changes to Delivery Service, queue, or router. Estimated effort: 1-2 days per channel."},{"area":"Notification Templates","evolution_path":"Template rendering is isolated inside each dispatcher. Can start with simple string interpolation and evolve to a template engine (Handlebars, Liquid) or even a template management service without touching the event flow."},{"area":"Queue Implementation","evolution_path":"The EventPublisher interface abstracts the queue. Can start with BullMQ on Redis for local development and swap to SQS in production, or migrate between queue technologies without touching business logic."},{"area":"Retry Policy","evolution_path":"Retry count, backoff strategy, and DLQ routing are queue configuration — changeable without code deployments. Can start with 3 retries with exponential backoff and tune based on observed provider reliability."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"DeliveryStatusChanged Event Schema","reason":"This is the contract between Delivery and Notification contexts. Changing it requires coordinated updates. Use a versioned schema from the start (include a 'version' field). This rigidity is intentional — stable contracts between bounded contexts are a feature, not a bug."},{"area":"At-Least-Once Delivery Semantics","reason":"The entire system is designed around at-least-once with idempotent consumers. Switching to exactly-once would require fundamental changes to queue choice and consumer logic. This is acceptable because at-least-once + idempotency is the proven pattern for this class of problem."},{"area":"Customer Preferences as Source of Truth for Channel Selection","reason":"The router dispatches based on stored preferences. If requirements evolve toward dynamic channel selection (e.g., 'use SMS only for urgent statuses'), the router logic needs extension. Design the router with a ChannelSelectionStrategy interface from the start to make this less painful."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Dual-write problem: delivery status saved but event not published, causing silent notification drops","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement the transactional outbox pattern. Write the event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the delivery status update. A poller publishes outbox rows to the queue. This guarantees zero silent drops."},{"risk":"Duplicate notifications sent to customers due to at-least-once delivery","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement idempotent consumers using (deliveryId, status, channel) as a deduplication key. Check before sending. Use a simple DB table or Redis SET with TTL."},{"risk":"Queue infrastructure outage causes notification backlog","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a managed queue service with built-in redundancy (SQS has 99.999% availability). The 5-minute latency budget provides natural buffer. DLQ + alerting catches anything that falls through."},{"risk":"External provider (SendGrid/Twilio) prolonged outage exhausts retries before recovery","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Configure generous retry policy (e.g., 5 retries over 30 minutes with exponential backoff). DLQ captures exhausted messages. Implement a re-drive capability to replay DLQ messages after provider recovery. This is a one-time script, not a full feature."},{"risk":"Team unfamiliarity with event-driven patterns leads to implementation errors","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Start with BullMQ (Node.js) or Celery (Python) — battle-tested libraries with excellent docs and built-in retry/DLQ support. Avoid building queue abstractions from scratch. Invest 1-2 days in a spike to validate the pattern before committing."},{"risk":"Outbox poller introduces slight latency overhead","severity":"low","mitigation":"5-10 second polling interval adds negligible latency against a 5-minute budget. Can optimize to push-based later if needed (it won't be)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): Implement the core event flow. Delivery service writes to outbox table on status change. Outbox poller publishes to queue (start with BullMQ on Redis or SQS). Single notification worker consumes events, resolves customer preferences, dispatches to email via SendGrid. Include idempotency check and DLQ with Slack alerting. Phase 2 (Week 3): Add SMS channel (Twilio dispatcher). This validates the extensibility of the channel architecture — should require zero changes to existing code. Phase 3 (Week 4): Harden. Add structured logging with correlation IDs (deliveryId as trace key). Add basic metrics (notifications sent, failed, retried per channel). Add DLQ re-drive script.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/delivery/events/DeliveryStatusChanged.ts — Domain event definition with version field","src/delivery/outbox/OutboxRepository.ts — Write events to outbox table in same transaction","src/delivery/outbox/OutboxPoller.ts — Polls outbox, publishes to queue, marks as published","src/notification/NotificationRouter.ts — Consumes events, resolves preferences, fans out to dispatchers","src/notification/dispatchers/Dispatcher.ts — Interface: send(payload): Result","src/notification/dispatchers/EmailDispatcher.ts — SendGrid integration","src/notification/dispatchers/SmsDispatcher.ts — Twilio integration","src/notification/dispatchers/ChannelRegistry.ts — Maps channelType to Dispatcher instance","src/notification/idempotency/IdempotencyCheck.ts — Dedup using (deliveryId, status, channel)","src/notification/preferences/CustomerPreferences.ts — Stores per-customer channel preferences","src/infrastructure/queue/EventPublisher.ts — Interface abstracting queue publish","src/infrastructure/queue/BullMQPublisher.ts — Concrete implementation","src/infrastructure/queue/QueueConsumer.ts — Consumer setup with retry and DLQ config","migrations/001_create_outbox_table.sql","migrations/002_create_idempotency_table.sql","migrations/003_create_notification_log_table.sql"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use dependency injection for all infrastructure concerns (queue, providers, DB). Every dispatcher implements the same Dispatcher interface — no special-casing. Correlation ID (deliveryId) must flow through every log line from event publication to provider API call. Keep the notification worker as a simple pipeline: deserialize → deduplicate → resolve preferences → dispatch → log. No business logic in infrastructure code.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build your own retry logic in application code — let the queue handle it. Application code should only signal retryable vs non-retryable failures.","Do NOT publish events directly from the delivery service to the queue without the outbox pattern — you WILL lose events under failure conditions.","Do NOT fan out to multiple queues per channel — use a single queue with the router handling fan-out. Multiple queues add operational burden for no benefit at this scale.","Do NOT use setTimeout or in-process scheduling for retries — these are lost on process restart.","Do NOT skip idempotency because 'duplicates are rare' — they are rare until they aren't, and duplicate SMS costs real money and customer trust.","Do NOT over-engineer the outbox poller — a simple setInterval polling loop with batch SELECT is sufficient. No need for change data capture."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Option B is clearly superior given the stated requirements (reliability > latency, extensibility needed, small team)","Managed queue over self-hosted broker for a 3-person team","Outbox pattern for solving the dual-write problem","Strategy pattern for channel extensibility","Idempotent consumer for at-least-once semantics"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology choice depends on team's existing infrastructure (AWS → SQS, already using Redis → BullMQ, GCP → Cloud Tasks) — cannot determine without knowing their stack","Whether the team's current skill set is Node.js or Python affects library recommendations"],"assumptions":["The team has access to a managed Redis or AWS SQS — they are not running bare metal","Notification volume is moderate (thousands/day, not millions) given courier company scale","The delivery service already has a relational database for storing delivery state","The team has basic experience with asynchronous processing patterns"]}}

No business logic in infrastructure code.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build your own retry logic in application code — let the queue handle it. Application code should only signal retryable vs non-retryable failures.","Do NOT publish events directly from the delivery service to the queue without the outbox pattern — you WILL lose events under failure conditions.","Do NOT fan out to multiple queues per channel — use a single queue with the router handling fan-out. Multiple queues add operational burden for no benefit at this scale.","Do NOT use setTimeout or in-process scheduling for retries — these are lost on process restart.","Do NOT skip idempotency because 'duplicates are rare' — they are rare until they aren't, and duplicate SMS costs real money and customer trust.","Do NOT over-engineer the outbox poller — a simple setInterval polling loop with batch SELECT is sufficient. No need for change data capture."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Option B is clearly superior given the stated requirements (reliability > latency, extensibility needed, small team)","Managed queue over self-hosted broker for a 3-person team","Outbox pattern for solving the dual-write problem","Strategy pattern for channel extensibility","Idempotent consumer for at-least-once semantics"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology choice depends on team's existing infrastructure (AWS → SQS, already using Redis → BullMQ, GCP → Cloud Tasks) — cannot determine without knowing their stack","Whether the team's current skill set is Node.js or Python affects library recommendations"],"assumptions":["The team has access to a managed Redis or AWS SQS — they are not running bare metal","Notification volume is moderate (thousands/day, not millions) given courier company scale","The delivery service already has a relational database for storing delivery state","The team has basic experience with asynchronous processing patterns"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier company (3 engineers) needing reliable, multi-channel delivery notifications with 5-minute latency tolerance, Option B (event-driven with a queue) is the clear winner. The latency budget removes the only advantage of synchronous calls, while the queue provides natural retry semantics, channel extensibility, and failure isolation — all critical requirements. However, the team size constraint means we must choose a managed queue (SQS, Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on Redis) rather than self-hosted Kafka or RabbitMQ. The architecture should be a simple delivery service publishing events to a managed queue, with per-channel notification workers consuming from it.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Owns delivery lifecycle. Publishes domain events (DeliveryStatusChanged) when a delivery transitions state. Knows nothing about notification channels.","boundaries":"Bounded by delivery domain only. Does not import or reference any notification logic. Publishes events through a thin EventPublisher interface."},{"name":"Event Queue (Managed)","responsibility":"Durable message broker that guarantees at-least-once delivery of domain events to notification workers. Handles retry with backoff, dead-letter routing, and visibility timeout.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. Accessed only through a thin adapter. AWS SQS, Google Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on managed Redis are appropriate choices for a 3-person team."},{"name":"Notification Router","responsibility":"Consumes domain events from the queue, resolves customer notification preferences (which channels), and fans out to per-channel dispatchers. This is a single lightweight worker process, not a separate service.","boundaries":"Owns the mapping from event type + customer preferences to channel dispatch. Does NOT own channel-specific formatting or API integration."},{"name":"Channel Dispatchers (Email, SMS, future: Push, WhatsApp)","responsibility":"Each dispatcher owns the integration with one external provider (SendGrid, Twilio, etc.), including message formatting, API calls, auth, and provider-specific error handling.","boundaries":"One module per channel. Stateless. Receives a normalized NotificationPayload and returns success/failure. Adding a new channel means adding one new dispatcher module — no changes to router or delivery service."},{"name":"Dead Letter Queue + Alert","responsibility":"Captures notifications that exhaust all retry attempts. Triggers an alert (PagerDuty, Slack webhook) so the team knows a customer was not notified.","boundaries":"Operational boundary. Messages here require manual investigation or automated re-drive."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Event Queue","contract":"Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged events: {deliveryId, customerId, status, timestamp, metadata}. Fire-and-forget from the service perspective.","coupling_type":"loose — delivery service depends only on an EventPublisher interface, not on queue implementation"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Notification Router","contract":"At-least-once delivery with visibility timeout. Router must be idempotent (use deliveryId + status as deduplication key).","coupling_type":"loose — queue is infrastructure, router polls or subscribes"},{"from":"Notification Router","to":"Channel Dispatchers","contract":"Calls dispatcher.send(NotificationPayload): {recipientId, channel, templateKey, templateData}. Returns {success: boolean, retryable: boolean, errorCode?}.","coupling_type":"loose — router depends on a Dispatcher interface; dispatchers are registered via a simple channel registry (map of channelType → dispatcher)"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Dead Letter Queue","contract":"Messages exceeding maxRetries are automatically routed to DLQ by the queue infrastructure.","coupling_type":"infrastructure-level — no application code needed"}],"data_flow":"1. Delivery service transitions a delivery to a new status → 2. Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged event to queue → 3. Notification Router consumes the event → 4. Router looks up customer notification preferences → 5. Router calls appropriate Channel Dispatcher(s) → 6. Dispatcher calls external provider API → 7. On failure: message returns to queue with backoff → 8. After max retries: message goes to DLQ and team is alerted","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Option B)","rationale":"The 5-minute latency tolerance eliminates synchronous advantages. A queue gives us: (1) natural retry with backoff — critical for the 'never silently drop' requirement, (2) channel decoupling — adding WhatsApp means adding a dispatcher, not modifying the delivery service, (3) failure isolation — if Twilio is down, email still works, (4) temporal decoupling — delivery service never blocks on slow provider APIs."},{"pattern":"Strategy Pattern for Channel Dispatchers","rationale":"Each channel is a strategy implementing a common Dispatcher interface. The Notification Router selects strategies based on customer preferences. Adding a channel is adding a class and registering it — Open/Closed Principle."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern (recommended addition)","rationale":"To prevent the dual-write problem (delivery status saved to DB but event lost, or vice versa), write the event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the delivery status change. A lightweight poller publishes outbox rows to the queue. This guarantees no events are silently lost — critical for the reliability requirement. For a 3-person team, a simple polling outbox (every 5-10 seconds) is sufficient; no need for CDC."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"After N retries, undeliverable messages go to a DLQ rather than being discarded. This is the safety net that guarantees the 'never silently dropped' requirement. Combined with a simple alert, this is operationally cheap."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Consumer","rationale":"At-least-once delivery means duplicates are possible. Using (deliveryId, status, channel) as an idempotency key prevents customers from receiving duplicate notifications. Simple DB check or in-memory cache with TTL."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, states: picked_up, in_transit, out_for_delivery, delivered, failed_attempt)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, tracks: channel, status, attempts, lastError)","CustomerPreferences (root: customerId, tracks: preferred channels, contact info per channel)"]},{"name":"Channel Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Stateless — no aggregates. Pure adapter layer wrapping external provider APIs."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Delivery","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Delivery publishes domain events; Notification consumes them. Delivery team defines the event schema. Notification team conforms to it but owns all notification logic."},{"upstream":"Notification","downstream":"Channel Integration","relationship":"Open Host Service. Notification defines the Dispatcher interface (the 'host'). Channel integrations conform to it. Each provider adapter is replaceable without affecting the notification context."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Option B (event-driven) over Option A (synchronous REST)","gains":"Retry is handled by queue infrastructure (not application code). Channels are decoupled — adding one is isolated. Delivery service is never blocked by slow/failing providers. Partial failures are graceful (email works even if SMS is down). Natural batching possible if volume grows.","sacrifices":"More moving parts than direct REST calls. Requires understanding message queue semantics (visibility timeout, DLQ, idempotency). Slightly harder to debug end-to-end (need correlation IDs and structured logging).","alternatives_considered":["Option A: Synchronous REST — simpler to build initially but retry logic becomes complex application code, delivery service couples to every channel, adding a channel means modifying the delivery service, provider outages block delivery processing. Rejected because the reliability requirement makes the retry problem hard to solve well in-process.","Hybrid: Synchronous with async fallback — adds complexity of both patterns without the clean benefits of either. Rejected for a 3-person team."]},{"decision":"Managed queue (SQS/BullMQ) over self-hosted broker (Kafka/RabbitMQ)","gains":"Zero operational overhead for the queue itself. No cluster management, no partition rebalancing, no disk monitoring. A 3-person team stays focused on business logic.","sacrifices":"Vendor lock-in to the chosen queue. Less control over advanced features (exactly-once, stream replay). SQS has 256KB message limit.","alternatives_considered":["Kafka — powerful but operationally heavy for 3 engineers and this volume. Overkill.","RabbitMQ — good middle ground but still requires cluster management, monitoring, upgrades. Unnecessary burden."]},{"decision":"Outbox pattern over direct publish-then-save","gains":"Guarantees event publication if the DB write succeeds. Eliminates the dual-write problem. No distributed transactions needed.","sacrifices":"Adds a polling loop and an outbox table. Slightly more code. Adds latency equal to poll interval (5-10s, well within the 5-minute budget).","alternatives_considered":["Publish-then-save — risk of DB failure after publish (customer notified about a status that wasn't saved). Unacceptable.","Save-then-publish — risk of publish failure after save (silent notification drop). Violates reliability requirement.","CDC (Debezium) — powerful but operationally complex for 3 engineers. Overkill."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Channel Dispatchers","evolution_path":"Adding push notifications or WhatsApp means: (1) implement Dispatcher interface for the new channel, (2) register it in the channel registry, (3) add it as an option in CustomerPreferences. No changes to Delivery Service, queue, or router. Estimated effort: 1-2 days per channel."},{"area":"Notification Templates","evolution_path":"Template rendering is isolated inside each dispatcher. Can start with simple string interpolation and evolve to a template engine (Handlebars, Liquid) or even a template management service without touching the event flow."},{"area":"Queue Implementation","evolution_path":"The EventPublisher interface abstracts the queue. Can start with BullMQ on Redis for local development and swap to SQS in production, or migrate between queue technologies without touching business logic."},{"area":"Retry Policy","evolution_path":"Retry count, backoff strategy, and DLQ routing are queue configuration — changeable without code deployments. Can start with 3 retries with exponential backoff and tune based on observed provider reliability."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"DeliveryStatusChanged Event Schema","reason":"This is the contract between Delivery and Notification contexts. Changing it requires coordinated updates. Use a versioned schema from the start (include a 'version' field). This rigidity is intentional — stable contracts between bounded contexts are a feature, not a bug."},{"area":"At-Least-Once Delivery Semantics","reason":"The entire system is designed around at-least-once with idempotent consumers. Switching to exactly-once would require fundamental changes to queue choice and consumer logic. This is acceptable because at-least-once + idempotency is the proven pattern for this class of problem."},{"area":"Customer Preferences as Source of Truth for Channel Selection","reason":"The router dispatches based on stored preferences. If requirements evolve toward dynamic channel selection (e.g., 'use SMS only for urgent statuses'), the router logic needs extension. Design the router with a ChannelSelectionStrategy interface from the start to make this less painful."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Dual-write problem: delivery status saved but event not published, causing silent notification drops","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement the transactional outbox pattern. Write the event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the delivery status update. A poller publishes outbox rows to the queue. This guarantees zero silent drops."},{"risk":"Duplicate notifications sent to customers due to at-least-once delivery","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement idempotent consumers using (deliveryId, status, channel) as a deduplication key. Check before sending. Use a simple DB table or Redis SET with TTL."},{"risk":"Queue infrastructure outage causes notification backlog","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a managed queue service with built-in redundancy (SQS has 99.999% availability). The 5-minute latency budget provides natural buffer. DLQ + alerting catches anything that falls through."},{"risk":"External provider (SendGrid/Twilio) prolonged outage exhausts retries before recovery","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Configure generous retry policy (e.g., 5 retries over 30 minutes with exponential backoff). DLQ captures exhausted messages. Implement a re-drive capability to replay DLQ messages after provider recovery. This is a one-time script, not a full feature."},{"risk":"Team unfamiliarity with event-driven patterns leads to implementation errors","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Start with BullMQ (Node.js) or Celery (Python) — battle-tested libraries with excellent docs and built-in retry/DLQ support. Avoid building queue abstractions from scratch. Invest 1-2 days in a spike to validate the pattern before committing."},{"risk":"Outbox poller introduces slight latency overhead","severity":"low","mitigation":"5-10 second polling interval adds negligible latency against a 5-minute budget. Can optimize to push-based later if needed (it won't be)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): Implement the core event flow. Delivery service writes to outbox table on status change. Outbox poller publishes to queue (start with BullMQ on Redis or SQS). Single notification worker consumes events, resolves customer preferences, dispatches to email via SendGrid. Include idempotency check and DLQ with Slack alerting. Phase 2 (Week 3): Add SMS channel (Twilio dispatcher). This validates the extensibility of the channel architecture — should require zero changes to existing code. Phase 3 (Week 4): Harden. Add structured logging with correlation IDs (deliveryId as trace key). Add basic metrics (notifications sent, failed, retried per channel). Add DLQ re-drive script.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/delivery/events/DeliveryStatusChanged.ts — Domain event definition with version field","src/delivery/outbox/OutboxRepository.ts — Write events to outbox table in same transaction","src/delivery/outbox/OutboxPoller.ts — Polls outbox, publishes to queue, marks as published","src/notification/NotificationRouter.ts — Consumes events, resolves preferences, fans out to dispatchers","src/notification/dispatchers/Dispatcher.ts — Interface: send(payload): Result","src/notification/dispatchers/EmailDispatcher.ts — SendGrid integration","src/notification/dispatchers/SmsDispatcher.ts — Twilio integration","src/notification/dispatchers/ChannelRegistry.ts — Maps channelType to Dispatcher instance","src/notification/idempotency/IdempotencyCheck.ts — Dedup using (deliveryId, status, channel)","src/notification/preferences/CustomerPreferences.ts — Stores per-customer channel preferences","src/infrastructure/queue/EventPublisher.ts — Interface abstracting queue publish","src/infrastructure/queue/BullMQPublisher.ts — Concrete implementation","src/infrastructure/queue/QueueConsumer.ts — Consumer setup with retry and DLQ config","migrations/001_create_outbox_table.sql","migrations/002_create_idempotency_table.sql","migrations/003_create_notification_log_table.sql"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use dependency injection for all infrastructure concerns (queue, providers, DB). Every dispatcher implements the same Dispatcher interface — no special-casing. Correlation ID (deliveryId) must flow through every log line from event publication to provider API call. Keep the notification worker as a simple pipeline: deserialize → deduplicate → resolve preferences → dispatch → log. No business logic in infrastructure code.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build your own retry logic in application code — let the queue handle it. Application code should only signal retryable vs non-retryable failures.","Do NOT publish events directly from the delivery service to the queue without the outbox pattern — you WILL lose events under failure conditions.","Do NOT fan out to multiple queues per channel — use a single queue with the router handling fan-out. Multiple queues add operational burden for no benefit at this scale.","Do NOT use setTimeout or in-process scheduling for retries — these are lost on process restart.","Do NOT skip idempotency because 'duplicates are rare' — they are rare until they aren't, and duplicate SMS costs real money and customer trust.","Do NOT over-engineer the outbox poller — a simple setInterval polling loop with batch SELECT is sufficient. No need for change data capture."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Option B is clearly superior given the stated requirements (reliability > latency, extensibility needed, small team)","Managed queue over self-hosted broker for a 3-person team","Outbox pattern for solving the dual-write problem","Strategy pattern for channel extensibility","Idempotent consumer for at-least-once semantics"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology choice depends on team's existing infrastructure (AWS → SQS, already using Redis → BullMQ, GCP → Cloud Tasks) — cannot determine without knowing their stack","Whether the team's current skill set is Node.js or Python affects library recommendations"],"assumptions":["The team has access to a managed Redis or AWS SQS — they are not running bare metal","Notification volume is moderate (thousands/day, not millions) given courier company scale","The delivery service already has a relational database for storing delivery state","The team has basic experience with asynchronous processing patterns"]}}
